feedback.text: Dear European Commission, thank you for taking the time to read my feedback. The Digital Fairness Act is a great step toward protecting consumers from the dangers of online media in the modern era. To further enhance its effectiveness, I would like to suggest placing additional emphesis on the current harmful practices employed by video game publishersparticularly concerning continued consumer access to video games once a publisher decides to end support. More and more publishers require an active internet connection with their servers in order for games to function, even when such connectivity is not technically necessary. This is a deliberate stratgy to ensure that nobody will continue to have access to their product after they decide to drop support. The games I am talking about here are not some typical consumer product that can simply be replaced. For many players, they are the source of impactful memories and the foundation for communities. They also hold historical significance, contributing to our collective cultural memory and serving as case studies for innovation in the video game industry. For that all to be taken away by actively preventing players from continuing to play these games, especially when doing so does not require continued first-party support, is detrimental to our shared digital heritage and the players for which many of these games constitute a large meaningful part of their lives. As a personal anecdote, I have played many online video games during my youth (couple of examples being Runescape, Guild Wars 2), and I like to return to them on occasion. While I am fortunate that all my favourite games are still actively supported by the publishers, nothing prevents them from pulling the plug some time in the future and preventing me from ever experiencing that timeslice in my life again. I would like to see the Digital Fairness Act include some sort of requirements for video game publishers to provide an end-of-life plan for their games, that ensures continued playability even without first-party support. The specific implementation details of such a plan can take many forms and can be left to the industry to find solutions to for. Even if it might not be possible to retro-actively apply this for games that are already released and have potentially lost first-party support, we can at least protect future games from being lost from our collective history at the whims of the publisher. Once again, thank you for considering my feedback, and good luck on the further implementation of this act.
